characters.relation: Twin brother; slain at the founding
characters.blurb: Twin of Romulus. Captured while raiding Amulius’s herds, then killed—by Romulus or by Celer—when he mocked the new walls.

characters.relation: Maternal grandfather; rightful king of Alba
characters.blurb: Deposed by Amulius, he recognized the twins and with them recovered Alba Longa before they founded Rome.

characters.relation: Usurping great-uncle at Alba
characters.blurb: Seized Numitor’s throne, forced Rhea Silvia into Vestal vows, and ordered the twins drowned. They returned and killed him.

characters.relation: Mother of the twins
characters.blurb: Daughter of Numitor, made a Vestal by Amulius. Mars (or a lover) fathered Romulus and Remus upon her.

characters.relation: Reputed divine father
characters.blurb: War-god said to have fathered the twins on Rhea Silvia—the pedigree Rome claimed for its martial destiny.

characters.relation: Shepherd foster-father
characters.blurb: Royal herdsman who found the twins at the fig-tree and raised them with Larentia. He later revealed their birth to Numitor.

characters.relation: Foster-mother
characters.blurb: Wife of Faustulus who nursed the twins. Some say “Lupa” was her nickname as a courtesan; others keep the wolf literal.

characters.relation: Sabine co-king
characters.blurb: King of the Sabines who warred on Rome after the rape of the women, then shared the throne with Romulus until he was murdered at Lavinium.

characters.relation: Wife; peacemaker with the Sabines
characters.blurb: Chief among the stolen Sabine women. She begged peace between fathers and husbands and later was said to share Romulus’s divine honors.

characters.relation: Betrayer of the Capitol
characters.blurb: Daughter of the citadel’s commander; she opened the gate to Tatius for what the Sabines wore on their left arms—and was crushed under their shields.

characters.relation: Commander of the Capitol
characters.blurb: Father of Tarpeia; entrusted with the Capitoline when the Sabines attacked. His daughter’s treachery cost Rome the rock.

characters.relation: King of Caenina; first spolia opima
characters.blurb: Ruler of Caenina who challenged Romulus after the rape. Romulus killed him and dedicated the first spolia opima to Jupiter Feretrius.

characters.relation: Wall-guard who may have killed Remus
characters.blurb: One tradition makes this swift lieutenant, not Romulus, the man who struck Remus for leaping the unfinished wall.

characters.relation: Early Sabine war casualty; ancestor of Tullus
characters.blurb: Roman who fell fighting the Sabines; his grandson Tullus Hostilius later became king. Plutarch marks the family among the first mixed houses.

characters.relation: Trojan ancestor of the Alban line
characters.blurb: Trojan exile whose Italian settlement founded the line of Alba Longa—Numitor, Rhea Silvia, and so the twins.

characters.relation: Earlier settler on the Palatine
characters.blurb: Arcadian who held the Palatine before Romulus; his rites of Hercules and the Lupercal color Rome’s oldest cults.

characters.relation: Hero honored at the Ara Maxima
characters.blurb: Evander’s guest-hero whose altar Romulus’s Rome kept. His cult marks the Greek strand in early Roman religion.

characters.relation: Divine name after his vanishing
characters.blurb: The god Romulus became when he disappeared in the storm—or so Proculus Julius swore to the mourning people.

characters.relation: Witness of Romulus’s apotheosis
characters.blurb: Patrician who swore Romulus met him on the road as Quirinus and ordered the Romans to cultivate arms and know their city would rule.

characters.relation: Name cried at Roman weddings
characters.blurb: Companion (or cry) tied to the rape of the Sabine women; Romans later shouted “Talasio” at marriages as a lucky omen.

characters.relation: Alternate Alban founder-tale king
characters.blurb: In one strange variant, an Alban king whose hearth-phallus story rivals the Mars-and-Rhea birth of the twins.

characters.relation: Husband in the Tarchetius variant
characters.blurb: Wealthy man married to a handmaid in the alternate birth story Tarchetius’s court produced.

characters.relation: Neighbor city Romulus took
characters.blurb: Latin town Romulus seized after it stirred against Rome—one of the early wars that widened his territory.

characters.relation: Etruscan neighbors; early foes
characters.blurb: People of Veii who fought Romulus after Fidenae fell. Their wars foreshadow Camillus’s later siege.

characters.relation: Historian cited on early Rome
characters.blurb: Learned king whose antiquarian notes Plutarch quotes among conflicting accounts of Rome’s beginnings.

characters.relation: Roman antiquarian cited
characters.blurb: Marcus Terentius Varro, whose chronology and lore Plutarch weighs against other founders’ dates.

characters.relation: Founder of Rome; subject of the Life
characters.blurb: Twin of Remus who traced descent from Mars, slew Remus and Acron, built Rome’s walls, and vanished in a storm to become the god Quirinus.

characters.relation: Chief god; recipient of the first spolia opima
characters.blurb: Romulus vowed his enemy Acron’s armor to Jupiter Feretrius after Caenina, and prayed to Jupiter Stator to halt the Roman rout in the Sabine war.

characters.relation: Prophetess; Evander’s mother
characters.blurb: Prophetic nymph whose oracles Evander carried to Italy; her name means either ‘verse-singer’ from carmen or ‘out of her mind’ for prophetic frenzy.

characters.relation: God whose temple held the brazen chariot
characters.blurb: Romulus placed a four-horse chariot from Camerium in the temple of Vulcan; that precinct was later one of the sites named for his disappearance.

characters.relation: Goddess who restrained Amulius; hearth of the city
characters.blurb: Vesta appeared to Amulius in a dream and dissuaded him from killing Rhea Silvia; her temple on the Sacred Way marked the Sabine truce.

characters.relation: Legendary alternate founder figure
characters.blurb: One variant makes Romus—son of Emathion or king of the Latins—the true eponym of Rome, displacing the Romulus-and-Remus tradition Plutarch examines.

characters.relation: Earliest Greek historian of Rome’s founding
characters.blurb: Author from Peparethus whom Fabius Pictor follows; together they are Plutarch’s oldest sources for the wolf, the twins, and the founding narrative.

characters.relation: Roman annalist; oldest Latin historian
characters.blurb: Wrote Rome’s founding traditions in Greek; Plutarch weighs his account alongside Diocles of Peparethus for the story of the twins and the Sabine war.

characters.relation: Second winner of the spolia opima
characters.blurb: Killed the Etruscan king Tolumnius in single combat and dedicated the second spolia opima to Jupiter Feretrius, following Romulus’s founding precedent.

characters.relation: Third and last winner of the spolia opima
characters.blurb: Slew the Gallic king Viridomarus and claimed the third spolia opima; later Caesar’s workmen, repairing steps near the trophy, reopened the inscription dispute.

characters.relation: Parallel figure: great man whose death was unexplained
characters.blurb: Plutarch invokes Scipio’s mysterious home-death to suggest that Romulus’s disappearance, however strange, is not unique among Rome’s greatest men.

characters.relation: Later figure cited in the spolia opima dispute
characters.blurb: Caius Caesar’s workmen, repairing steps near an ancient trophy site, uncovered an inscription that reopened debate about Cossus’s spolia opima claim.

characters.relation: Peaceable successor mentioned in the Life
characters.blurb: Plutarch notes that the Roman calendar and Vestal order were disputed between Romulus and Numa—the martial founder and the priestly king differ in Plutarch’s comparison.

characters.relation: Greek mythic figure who vanished like Romulus
characters.blurb: Died in a fuller’s workshop yet was later seen traveling; Plutarch pairs him with Cleomedes as Greek parallels to Romulus’s miraculous translation.

characters.relation: Greek hero who vanished; parallel to Romulus
characters.blurb: Mighty and wild athlete from Astypalaea who disappeared after a mad act—one of the Greek vanishing-hero tales Plutarch places beside Romulus’s translation.

characters.relation: Historian who recorded the Tarpeia tradition
characters.blurb: Cited for the version that Tarpeius arranged his daughter’s treachery himself—a reading Plutarch considers absurd but records among the conflicting accounts.

characters.relation: Latin commander who menaced Rome after the Gallic sack
characters.blurb: Livius Postumius led a Latin army against the weakened city; Plutarch records his demand for Roman wives and the ruse of the handmaids that saved Rome.

characters.relation: Historian of Rome; cited in triumph dispute
characters.blurb: Dionysius of Halicarnassus, Greek historian of Rome; Plutarch explicitly corrects him for asserting Romulus rode in a chariot at his triumph—statues of Romulus in Rome show him on foot.

characters.relation: Corinthian exile; Tarquinius Priscus's father
characters.blurb: Wealthy Corinthian who emigrated to Etruria and fathered Tarquinius Priscus; mentioned when Plutarch notes that Tarquinius—not Romulus—was the first to surround the triumph with great pomp.

characters.relation: Fifth king of Rome; gave triumphs their pomp
characters.blurb: Tarquinius Priscus, Damaratus's son and fifth king of Rome; one tradition credits him—rather than Romulus or Publicola—with introducing the magnificent trappings that made the Roman triumph the spectacle it became.

characters.relation: Etruscan king killed by Cossus; second spolia opima
characters.blurb: King of Veii whose death at the hands of Cornelius Cossus in single combat earned the second spolia opima in Roman history—following the precedent Romulus set by killing Acron.

characters.relation: Gallic king killed by Marcellus; third spolia opima
characters.blurb: Gallic chieftain slain by Claudius Marcellus in single combat—earning the third and last spolia opima ever dedicated, completing the trio begun by Romulus's victory over Acron.

characters.relation: Aeneas's son; mentioned in Rome's founding myths
characters.blurb: Son of Aeneas; one variant of the founding myth makes Roma—the city's eponym—a Trojan lady married to Ascanius, placing Rome's origin in the Aeneid's direct male line.

characters.relation: Mythographer who noted Hercules and the vulture
characters.blurb: Heraclean mythographer who recorded that Hercules rejoiced whenever a vulture appeared to him at the start of any undertaking—cited to explain why Romans regard the vulture as the premier omen-bird in divination.

characters.relation: Author of a history of Italy; Tarchetius story
characters.blurb: Compiled a History of Italy in which he recorded the strange birth-legend of Tarchetius the Alban king—the rival founding myth involving a hearth-phallus that Plutarch presents as an alternative to the Mars-and-Rhea tradition.

characters.relation: Tarchetius's agent who spared the twins
characters.blurb: The man to whom Tarchetius handed the twin infants with orders to destroy them; instead Teratius carried them to the river bank, where a wolf suckled them until a cowherd found them.

characters.relation: Faustulus's brother; foster-uncle of Romulus
characters.blurb: Brother of the shepherd Faustulus who helped raise Romulus and Remus; he too was killed in the scuffle at the new city's walls when Remus leaped over them in contempt.

characters.relation: Teian poet whose eclipse fixes Rome's founding date
characters.blurb: Archaic poet of Teos; the solar eclipse he recorded in the third year of the sixth Olympiad is the astronomical anchor Plutarch mentions for the traditional date on which Romulus laid Rome's first stone.

characters.relation: Roman commander who gained the nickname Celer
characters.blurb: Quintus Metellus who earned the cognomen Celer by producing a gladiatorial show in record time after his father's funeral—cited to explain how the Roman word Celeres, originally Romulus's bodyguard, became a general term for swiftness.

characters.relation: Handmaid who saved Rome from the Latin ruse
characters.blurb: A servant-maid (called Philotis or, in another version, Tutola) who devised the stratagem of sending maid-servants disguised as free virgins to the Latin commander Postumius, then lit a torch as a signal for the Romans to fall on him.

characters.relation: Poet who wrongly said Tarpeia betrayed to the Gauls
characters.blurb: Poet who spun a fanciful variant in which Tarpeia was in love with a Gallic king rather than with Sabine gold—an error Plutarch dismisses as 'mere folly.'

characters.relation: Roman historian cited by Juba on Tarpeius
characters.blurb: Roman writer whose account of Tarpeius being found guilty of treason is preserved through Juba; Plutarch invokes him to show that the father of the treacherous Tarpeia was himself prosecuted and convicted.

characters.relation: Thracian prince; recipient of Caesar's famous quip
characters.blurb: A Thracian prince to whom Julius Caesar reportedly said he loved the treason but hated the traitor—cited alongside Antigonus's identical saying to illuminate the universal contempt for Tarpeia even among those who benefited from her betrayal.

characters.relation: First parricide in Roman history
characters.blurb: Roman citizen recorded as the first person ever to commit parricide in Rome—after nearly six centuries in which no such crime occurred, his act came only after the Hannibalic wars shattered the old moral fabric.

characters.relation: Playwright quoted for eagles killing their own kind
characters.blurb: Athenian tragedian; Plutarch quotes his verse about eagles and owls preying on creatures of their own species to explain why Romans prefer the vulture as an omen-bird—it alone never kills its kindred.

characters.relation: Aeneas's wife in the Alban genealogy
characters.blurb: Daughter of the Latin king Latinus who married Aeneas and gave her name to Lavinium; in one founding myth the twins' line runs through Aemilia, who was Aeneas's daughter by Lavinia.

characters.relation: Co-founder of the Republic; first to ride in triumph
characters.blurb: Publius Valerius Publicola, Rome's great republican lawgiver; one tradition credits him rather than Tarquinius with first riding in triumph—cited when Plutarch surveys conflicting accounts of the ceremony Romulus invented on foot.

characters.relation: Dexithea's father in one founding legend
characters.blurb: In one variant of the founding myth, Dexithea daughter of Phorbas was the mother of Romulus by Aeneas—a genealogy connecting the Latin founder tradition to an otherwise obscure Greek name.

characters.relation: Historian who gave an account of Tarpeia
characters.blurb: Historian from Troezen who told a version of the Tarpeia story; Plutarch cites him among the sources whose accounts of the Capitol's betrayal he weighs and largely rejects.

characters.relation: Phorbas's daughter; a legendary mother of Romulus
characters.blurb: In one founding variant, daughter of Phorbas and the woman Aeneas took as the mother of Romulus—an alternative birth-legend preserved among the many conflicting traditions Plutarch catalogues at the opening of the Life.

characters.relation: Legendary daughter of Aeneas; mother of Romulus in one myth
characters.blurb: In another founding variant, Aemilia—daughter of Aeneas by Lavinia—was said to have conceived Romulus by the god Mars; a tradition linking Rome's founder through Lavinia's lineage directly to Aeneas and divine parentage.

characters.relation: Son of Telemachus; Roma's husband in one legend
characters.blurb: In one founding variant, Roma daughter of the Trojan woman was married to Latinus—son of Telemachus—and became the mother of Romulus; a genealogy threading the Romans back through Ulysses as well as Aeneas.

characters.relation: Son of Odysseus; Latinus's father in one founding myth
characters.blurb: Homer's Telemachus appears in one alternative founding legend as the father of Latinus who wed Roma—a tradition connecting Rome's origins to the Odyssey's hero as well as the Iliad's Aeneas.

locations.blurb: Royal Alba of Numitor and Amulius—where the twins were born before the basket on the Tiber.
Set adrift, caught at the flood’s edge by Faustulus—Rome’s river as cradle before the Palatine.
He chose the Palatine for the new city—walls, rites, and the quarrel that killed Remus.
Named for him in the legend—asylum, tribes, and the calendar of a city that outgrew the hill.
Among the first towns he took in the wars that fed Rome’s strength and Sabine entanglement.
Fidenae felt the new city’s arms—another step in Romulus’s ring of conquest around the Tiber.

locations.relation: Hill of the asylum and Jupiter
locations.blurb: He opened an asylum on the Capitol—refuge that swelled Rome’s people and its peril.
Remus took the Aventine for his birds—the rival hill in the founding omen.
City of Aeneas and Lavinia in the ancestry that links Troy’s remnant to Alba and Rome.

locations.relation: Latin coast of the Aeneas tale
locations.blurb: Named with Lavinium in the Latin shore-story behind Romulus’s Alban line.

locations.relation: Latin town in early Rome’s orbit
locations.blurb: Counted among Latin neighbors as Rome’s power thickened east of the city.
